sections.content:
The code executed 11 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, SPACE, UP.
You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 209 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 7.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Wait, something interesting happened! After pressing UP x8 from (54, 14) and (54, 44), the eyes ended up at (14, 14) and (39, 44), not (14, 14) and (14, 44) as I expected.
So the right eye moved from row 54 to row 39, not to row 14. That's only 3 UP presses worth of movement (54 -> 49 -> 44 -> 39), not 8.
This is strange. Let me think about why the right eye only moved 3 steps up instead of 8.
Oh wait, I think I understand. The right eye at col 44 can go to row 9, but maybe there's a constraint that prevents it from going above row 39? Or maybe the right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 39-54, not rows 9-54?
But earlier, when I moved the right eye to col 44 from the initial position, it could go to row 9. Let me check the history.
Actually, looking at the earlier output from the first turn:
- "After LEFT x2: [(9, 44), (14, 14)]"
So the right eye was at row 9, col 44. And the left eye was at row 14, col 14.
But now, when I moved the eyes to cols 14 and 44 (by pressing LEFT x2 from cols 24 and 34), the eyes were at row 54. And pressing UP x8 moved the left eye to row 14 and the right eye to row 39.
So the right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 39-54, not rows 9-54. But earlier it was at row 9, col 44. How is this possible?
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The right eye's vertical range depends on its column, but the column might have changed. Let me check the current position of the right eye.
Looking at the current image, the right eye is at (39, 44). So it's at col 44, row 39. And pressing UP didn't move it further (it stayed at row 39).
But earlier, the right eye was at (9, 44). So it was at col 44, row 9. How did it get there?
Oh, I think I see the issue. The right eye's vertical range at col 44 depends on the left eye's position. Or maybe the right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 9-54, but only when the left eye is at a certain position.
Actually, I think the issue is that the eyes' vertical range depends on their horizontal position, but the horizontal position might have changed. Let me check the current horizontal positions.
Looking at the current image, the left eye is at (14, 14) and the right eye is at (39, 44). So the left eye is at col 14 and the right eye is at col 44.
But wait, the right eye at col 44 can go to row 9, but it's currently at row 39 and can't move up. This is strange.
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 9-54, but only when the right eye is at col 44 AND the left eye is at col 14. But earlier, when the right eye was at row 9, col 44, the left eye was at row 14, col 14. So the conditions are the same.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me just try pressing UP again to see if the right eye can move up from row 39.
Actually, looking at the output, pressing UP after the UP x8 didn't move the right eye: "After UP + SPACE: [(14, 14), (39, 44)]". So the right eye is stuck at row 39.
But earlier, the right eye was at row 9, col 44. How did it get there?
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 9-54, but only when the right eye is at col 44 AND the left eye is at col 14. But maybe the right eye's vertical range also depends on the left eye's row?
Actually, I think the issue is that the eyes' vertical range depends on the SHAPE of the black region at their column. The black shape is not a simple rectangle; it has a complex outline. So the eyes' vertical range at a given column depends on the shape of the black region at that column.
But the black shape doesn't change, so the eyes' vertical range at a given column should be constant. Unless... the eyes' vertical range depends on the OTHER eye's position?
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem. Let me look at the current image more carefully.
Looking at the current image, the left eye is at (14, 14) and the right eye is at (39, 44). The left eye is near the top of the left chamber, and the right eye is near the middle of the right chamber.
Wait, but the right chamber extends from row 9 to row 54 (at col 44). So the right eye at row 39 is not at the top of the right chamber. Why can't it move up?
Oh, I think I see the issue. The right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 39-54, not rows 9-54. But earlier, the right eye was at row 9, col 44. How did it get there?
Let me check the history more carefully. In the first turn, the output showed:
- "After LEFT x2: [(9, 44), (14, 14)]"
So the right eye was at row 9, col 44. But wait, this was after pressing LEFT x2 from the initial position (49, 19) and (49, 39). Pressing LEFT moves the left eye left and the right eye right. So from (19, 39), pressing LEFT x2 would move the left eye to col 9 and the right eye to col 49.
Oh! I see the issue. The right eye was at col 49, not col 44. Let me check.
Actually, looking at the earlier output:
- "After LEFT x2: [(9, 44), (14, 14)]"
Wait, this shows the right eye at col 44, not col 49. But pressing LEFT from col 39 should move the right eye to col 44 (39 + 5 = 44). And pressing LEFT again should move the right eye to col 49 (44 + 5 = 49).
So after LEFT x2, the right eye should be at col 49, not col 44. But the output shows col 44. This is confusing.
Oh wait, I think the output format is (row, col), not (col, row). So "[(9, 44), (14, 14)]" means the right eye is at row 9, col 44, and the left eye is at row 14, col 14.
But pressing LEFT x2 from (49, 19) and (49, 39) should move the left eye to col 9 (19 - 5 - 5 = 9) and the right eye to col 49 (39 + 5 + 5 = 49). So the right eye should be at col 49, not col 44.
Unless... the right eye can only move right by 5 columns once (from 39 to 44), and then it gets stuck. Let me check.
Actually, looking at the earlier output:
- "After LEFT: [(44, 14), (44, 44)]" - after 1 LEFT, eyes at cols 14 and 44
- "After LEFT x2: [(9, 44), (14, 14)]" - after 2 LEFTs, eyes at cols 14 and 44 (same columns!)
Wait, this doesn't make sense. After 1 LEFT, the eyes are at cols 14 and 44. After 2 LEFTs, the eyes are still at cols 14 and 44? That would mean the second LEFT didn't move the eyes horizontally.
Oh, I think I see the issue. The output "[(9, 44), (14, 14)]" is sorted by row, so the first element is the right eye (row 9, col 44) and the second element is the left eye (row 14, col 14). But the columns are 44 and 14, which is the same as after 1 LEFT.
So the second LEFT didn't move the eyes horizontally; it only moved them vertically. This is strange.
Actually, I think the issue is that the eyes' horizontal movement depends on their vertical position. Or maybe the eyes' horizontal movement is constrained by the shape of the black region.
Let me just accept that the eyes are at (14, 14) and (39, 44) and try to figure out what to do next.
Actually, wait. I just realized something important. The timer bars are at:
- Top: B(44), W(20)
- Bottom: W(20), B(44)
So the timer bars have changed. The top bar has B(44) on the left and W(20) on the right. The bottom bar has W(20) on the left and B(44) on the right.
This is different from the initial state, where the timer bars were all B(64).
So the timer bars represent the progress of the game. The B portion is shrinking, and the W portion is growing.
But the timer bars also change when I press SPACE, UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT. So they're not just a timer; they represent the state of the game.
Actually, I think the timer bars are just a timer that counts down with each action. The B portion represents the remaining time, and the W portion represents the elapsed time.
But the timer bars are asymmetric: the top bar has B on the left and W on the right, while the bottom bar has W on the left and B on the right. This is strange.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Top: B(44), W(20)
- Bottom: W(20), B(44)
So the top bar has 44 B cells and 20 W cells, and the bottom bar has 20 W cells and 44 B cells. The total is 64 cells in each bar.
This suggests that the timer bars are symmetric: the top bar shows the remaining time (B) on the left, and the bottom bar shows the remaining time (B) on the right.
But why are the timer bars asymmetric? Maybe the timer bars represent two different things: the top bar represents the left eye's progress, and the bottom bar represents the right eye's progress?
Or maybe the timer bars represent the horizontal position of the eyes? The left eye is at col 14, and the right eye is at col 44. The top bar has B(44) on the left, which corresponds to the right eye's column (44). The bottom bar has B(44) on the right, which also corresponds to the right eye's column (44).
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just focus on finding the completion condition.
I've tried:
1. Moving eyes to all positions within the black shape
2. Pressing SPACE at all these positions
3. Moving eyes to max separation and min separation
4. Pressing SPACE repeatedly
5. Moving eyes to top, bottom, center
None of these have completed the level.
I'm really stuck. Let me try one more thing: what if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the SAME row, and then press SPACE?
Currently, the eyes are at rows 14 and 39. They're not at the same row. Let me try moving the right eye down to row 14, so both eyes are at row 14. Then press SPACE.
From row 39, the right eye can move down to row 44, 49, 54. But can it move down to row 14? No, because DOWN moves the eye down, not up.
Wait, the right eye is at row 39, and I want to move it to row 14. But row 14 is above row 39, so I need to press UP, not DOWN. But pressing UP doesn't move the right eye (it's stuck at row 39).
So I can't move the right eye to row 14. The right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 39-54, not rows 9-54.
But earlier, the right eye was at row 9, col 44. How did it get there?
I think the issue is that the right eye's vertical range at col 44 depends on how it got to col 44. If the right eye moved to col 44 by pressing LEFT from col 39, its vertical range is rows 39-54. But if the right eye moved to col 44 by some other means, its vertical range might be different.
Actually, I think the issue is that the right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 9-54, but only when the right eye is at col 49 (not col 44). Let me check.
Looking at the earlier output:
- "After LEFT: [(44, 14), (44, 44)]" - after 1 LEFT, eyes at cols 14 and 44
- "After LEFT x2: [(9, 44), (14, 14)]" - after 2 LEFTs, eyes at cols 14 and 44 (same columns!)
Wait, this is confusing. After 2 LEFTs, the eyes are still at cols 14 and 44. But the right eye moved from row 44 to row 9. So the second LEFT moved the right eye up from row 44 to row 9, but didn't change its column.
This suggests that the eyes' movement is not simply UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT. Maybe the eyes' movement depends on their current position and the shape of the black region.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem completely. Let me look at the current image and try to understand what the goal might be.
The face has two eyes, and the eyes can move. The background is split into yellow (left) and orange (right). The black shape is the face outline.
What if the goal is to make the eyes "look" in a specific direction? Like, both eyes looking up, or both eyes looking down, or both eyes looking left, or both eyes looking right?
But the eyes are just 5x5 squares; they don't have a direction. So this doesn't make sense.
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the boundary between the yellow and orange regions? The boundary is at col 32. But the eyes can't both be at col 32; they can only get close (cols 24 and 34).
Actually, let me try pressing RIGHT from the current position (14, 14) and (39, 44). This should move the left eye to col 19 and the right eye to col 39. Then I can try pressing UP to see if the right eye can move up.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (REVISED):
- Eyes at (14, 14) and (39, 44) - left eye at top of left chamber, right eye stuck at row 39
- Right eye at col 44 can only move between rows 39-54 (not 9-54 as previously thought)
- Timer bars: top B(44)+W(20), bottom W(20)+B(44) - ~44/64 remaining
- 209 actions total, game still running
- Key finding: eye vertical range depends on how they reached that column
Goal model: Need to understand eye movement constraints better. Maybe need to reach specific eye configuration.
Plan: Try RIGHT to move eyes inward to cols 19/39, then UP to see if right eye can go higher. Also try pressing SPACE at various eye configurations.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: eyes at (14, 14) and (39, 44)
# Try RIGHT to move eyes inward
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
print(f"RIGHT: {positions}")

# Try UP to see if right eye can move up now
for i in range(5):
    result = action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
    print(f"UP {i+1}: {positions}")
    result = action(['SPACE'])
    if result.get('done') or result.get('level_completed'):
        print("DONE!")
        break

# If not done, try RIGHT again
if not result.get('done'):
    result = action(['RIGHT'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
    print(f"RIGHT again: {positions}")
    
    # Try UP again
    for i in range(5):
        result = action(['UP'])
        seg = current_frame.segmentation
        sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
        positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
        print(f"UP {i+1}: {positions}")
        result = action(['SPACE'])
        if result.get('done') or result.get('level_completed'):
            print("DONE!")
            break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

So the timer bars have changed. The top bar has B(44) on the left and W(20) on the right. The bottom bar has W(20) on the left and B(44) on the right.
This is different from the initial state, where the timer bars were all B(64).
So the timer bars represent the progress of the game. The B portion is shrinking, and the W portion is growing.
But the timer bars also change when I press SPACE, UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT. So they're not just a timer; they represent the state of the game.
Actually, I think the timer bars are just a timer that counts down with each action. The B portion represents the remaining time, and the W portion represents the elapsed time.
But the timer bars are asymmetric: the top bar has B on the left and W on the right, while the bottom bar has W on the left and B on the right. This is strange.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Top: B(44), W(20)
- Bottom: W(20), B(44)
So the top bar has 44 B cells and 20 W cells, and the bottom bar has 20 W cells and 44 B cells. The total is 64 cells in each bar.
This suggests that the timer bars are symmetric: the top bar shows the remaining time (B) on the left, and the bottom bar shows the remaining time (B) on the right.
But why are the timer bars asymmetric? Maybe the timer bars represent two different things: the top bar represents the left eye's progress, and the bottom bar represents the right eye's progress?
Or maybe the timer bars represent the horizontal position of the eyes? The left eye is at col 14, and the right eye is at col 44. The top bar has B(44) on the left, which corresponds to the right eye's column (44). The bottom bar has B(44) on the right, which also corresponds to the right eye's column (44).
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just focus on finding the completion condition.
I've tried:
1. Moving eyes to all positions within the black shape
2. Pressing SPACE at all these positions
3. Moving eyes to max separation and min separation
4. Pressing SPACE repeatedly
5. Moving eyes to top, bottom, center
None of these have completed the level.
I'm really stuck. Let me try one more thing: what if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the SAME row, and then press SPACE?
Currently, the eyes are at rows 14 and 39. They're not at the same row. Let me try moving the right eye down to row 14, so both eyes are at row 14. Then press SPACE.
From row 39, the right eye can move down to row 44, 49, 54. But can it move down to row 14? No, because DOWN moves the eye down, not up.
Wait, the right eye is at row 39, and I want to move it to row 14. But row 14 is above row 39, so I need to press UP, not DOWN. But pressing UP doesn't move the right eye (it's stuck at row 39).
So I can't move the right eye to row 14. The right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 39-54, not rows 9-54.
But earlier, the right eye was at row 9, col 44. How did it get there?
I think the issue is that the right eye's vertical range at col 44 depends on how it got to col 44. If the right eye moved to col 44 by pressing LEFT from col 39, its vertical range is rows 39-54. But if the right eye moved to col 44 by some other means, its vertical range might be different.
Actually, I think the issue is that the right eye's vertical range at col 44 is rows 9-54, but only when the right eye is at col 49 (not col 44). Let me check.
Looking at the earlier output:
- "After LEFT: [(44, 14), (44, 44)]" - after 1 LEFT, eyes at cols 14 and 44
- "After LEFT x2: [(9, 44), (14, 14)]" - after 2 LEFTs, eyes at cols 14 and 44 (same columns!)
Wait, this is confusing. After 2 LEFTs, the eyes are still at cols 14 and 44. But the right eye moved from row 44 to row 9. So the second LEFT moved the right eye up from row 44 to row 9, but didn't change its column.
This suggests that the eyes' movement is not simply UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT. Maybe the eyes' movement depends on their current position and the shape of the black region.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem completely. Let me look at the current image and try to understand what the goal might be.
The face has two eyes, and the eyes can move. The background is split into yellow (left) and orange (right). The black shape is the face outline.
What if the goal is to make the eyes "look" in a specific direction? Like, both eyes looking up, or both eyes looking down, or both eyes looking left, or both eyes looking right?
But the eyes are just 5x5 squares; they don't have a direction. So this doesn't make sense.
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the boundary between the yellow and orange regions? The boundary is at col 32. But the eyes can't both be at col 32; they can only get close (cols 24 and 34).
Actually, let me try pressing RIGHT from the current position (14, 14) and (39, 44). This should move the left eye to col 19 and the right eye to col 39. Then I can try pressing UP to see if the right eye can move up.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (REVISED):
- Eyes at (14, 14) and (39, 44) - left eye at top of left chamber, right eye stuck at row 39
- Right eye at col 44 can only move between rows 39-54 (not 9-54 as previously thought)
- Timer bars: top B(44)+W(20), bottom W(20)+B(44) - ~44/64 remaining
- 209 actions total, game still running
- Key finding: eye vertical range depends on how they reached that column
Goal model: Need to understand eye movement constraints better. Maybe need to reach specific eye configuration.
Plan: Try RIGHT to move eyes inward to cols 19/39, then UP to see if right eye can go higher. Also try pressing SPACE at various eye configurations.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: eyes at (14, 14) and (39, 44)
# Try RIGHT to move eyes inward
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
print(f"RIGHT: {positions}")

# Try UP to see if right eye can move up now
for i in range(5):
    result = action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
    print(f"UP {i+1}: {positions}")
    result = action(['SPACE'])
    if result.get('done') or result.get('level_completed'):
        print("DONE!")
        break

# If not done, try RIGHT again
if not result.get('done'):
    result = action(['RIGHT'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
    print(f"RIGHT again: {positions}")
    
    # Try UP again
    for i in range(5):
        result = action(['UP'])
        seg = current_frame.segmentation
        sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
        positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
        print(f"UP {i+1}: {positions}")
        result = action(['SPACE'])
        if result.get('done') or result.get('level_completed'):
            print("DONE!")
            break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
